extract_mft_timeline: Returns file system activity timeline from MFT with SI/FN timestamp separation for timestomp detection. Use to find when files were created, modified, or accessed -- look for tool staging in temp directories. Capped at 5000 records; narrow start/end (YYYY-MM-DD) to focus on attack window.
get_amcache: Returns program execution history from disk (path, SHA1, first run timestamp). Use during investigation to confirm a process actually ran -- corroborates memory findings and cross-domain evidence upgrades confidence to HIGH. If empty, AmCache may have been cleared as anti-forensics.
get_investigation_categories: Call this FIRST to discover available tools and their required arguments. Returns tool names, descriptions, and parameter schemas grouped by 8 investigation categories. Use this before selecting additional tools in Step 5.
get_tools_for_category: Returns all available tools for a specific investigation category (e.g. 'malware_detection', 'network_analysis'). Call tool_get_investigation_categories first to see valid category names. If the category is invalid, returns an error -- do not guess category names.
list_volatility_plugins: Returns all available Volatility 3 windows plugin names (104+). Use to discover plugins before calling tool_run_volatility. Only needed for plugins without a dedicated wrapper -- check specific tools first (tool_vol_pstree, tool_vol_netscan, etc.).
parse_event_logs: Returns Windows event log entries (Security, System, Application). Look for Event ID 4688 (process creation), 4624 (logon), 7045 (service install) to corroborate memory findings. Capped at 2000 records; if empty, logs may have been cleared (check for Event ID 1102).
parse_prefetch: Returns recently executed programs with run counts and timestamps from disk Prefetch files. Use during investigation to confirm process execution history -- complements amcache for cross-domain corroboration. If empty, Prefetch may be disabled or files deleted as anti-forensics.
parse_shellbags: Returns folder access history from Shellbags, showing directories the user browsed. Use to prove attacker navigated to sensitive folders (e.g. HR, finance, M&A). If empty, SBECmd CSV output may not exist at the expected path -- check disk mount.
recommend_tools: Returns recommended tools for a natural-language forensic question (e.g. 'What processes are hiding?'). Use when you have an investigation hypothesis but are unsure which tools to run. Prefer this over browsing categories when you have a specific question to answer.
run_bulk_extractor: Carves emails, URLs, domains, and credit card numbers from disk/memory images. Use for C2 URL discovery and data exfiltration analysis -- returns counts per type plus top results. Long-running on large images; prefer targeted tools (netscan, amcache) for quick checks.
run_exiftool: Returns file metadata as JSON (timestamps, author, embedded paths, compile time). Use on suspicious executables to check compile timestamps and PE metadata. If metadata is sparse, the file may be packed or have had metadata stripped.
run_foremost: Carves files by header/footer signatures from disk or memory images (JPG, PDF, ZIP, EXE, etc). Use to recover deleted files or find hidden data in unallocated space. Output goes to output_dir -- check subdirectories per file type for carved results.
run_log2timeline: Generates a Plaso super-timeline from evidence, combining all timestamp sources into one chronological view. WARNING: long-running (up to 30 min on large images). Prefer tool_extract_mft_timeline or tool_parse_event_logs for quick targeted queries -- use this only when full timeline correlation is needed.
run_regripper: Parses Windows registry hives for forensic artifacts (persistence, user activity, system config). Use optional plugin filter for targeted extraction (e.g. 'compname', 'services', 'userassist'). If empty, the hive path may be wrong -- use tool_vol_reg_hivelist to find correct paths.
run_sleuthkit: Runs a Sleuthkit command against a disk image (14 allowed: fls, icat, mmls, blkstat, fsstat, ifind, ffind, mactime, sorter, sigfind, img_stat, img_cat, tsk_recover, tsk_loaddb). Use fls for directory listing, icat for file extraction, mmls for partition layout. If the command is not in the allowed list, it will be rejected.
run_ssdeep: Returns a fuzzy hash (ssdeep) for file similarity comparison. Use to cluster related malware variants or detect slightly modified attacker tools. Compare hashes between files -- a high match score (>50%) indicates the files share significant content.
run_strings: Extracts printable strings from binary files (encoding: 'unicode' or 'ascii', max 5000 results). Use to find C2 URLs, embedded commands, or attacker messages in suspicious executables. If results are sparse, try the alternate encoding -- malware often uses one or the other.
run_volatility: Runs any Volatility 3 plugin by name (104 available, must start with 'windows.'). Use tool_list_volatility_plugins to discover plugin names. Prefer specific tools (tool_vol_pstree, etc.) when available -- use this only for plugins without a dedicated wrapper.
run_yara: Runs YARA rules against files or directories, scanning recursively for IOC matches. Use for threat hunting when you have specific signatures to search for. If no matches are returned, the rules did not match -- this is a valid negative result, not a tool failure.
vol_cmdline: Returns command line arguments for each process. Use to identify encoded PowerShell, suspicious arguments, or empty cmdlines (indicator of process hollowing). If empty for a specific PID, the process may have cleared its PEB -- treat as suspicious, not a tool failure.
vol_dlllist: Returns loaded DLLs per process with paths and sizes. Use to verify executable paths and find unsigned or unusual DLLs (non-standard path = potential hijack). Returns thousands of records on healthy profiles -- always filter by PID during investigation.
vol_envars: Returns environment variables per process (USERNAME, PATH, TEMP). Use to determine execution context and identify processes running under unexpected user accounts. Filter by PID during investigation; unfiltered output is large and rarely useful.
vol_filescan: Returns all file objects referenced in memory, including deleted or hidden files not visible on disk. Use to find attacker tools or exfiltrated data that was deleted after use. Large result set -- search output by keyword rather than reading all records.
vol_getsids: Returns security identifiers per process, mapping PIDs to user/group identity. Use to check if processes run as SYSTEM vs user-level -- a user-level process with SYSTEM SID is suspicious. Filter by PID; if empty for a process, SID structures may be corrupted.
vol_handles: Returns open handles per process: files, registry keys, mutexes, named pipes. Use to reveal attacker file access and named pipe C2 channels (for example, anomalous beacon-like pipe names). Always filter by PID -- unfiltered output is massive and wastes tokens.
vol_malfind: Returns suspicious memory regions with injected code indicators (PAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE). High false positive rate from security tools (McAfee, Dashlane) -- corroborate with netscan or cmdline before elevating confidence. If empty, no VAD anomalies detected (good sign, not a tool failure).
vol_mftscan: Returns MFT entries carved from raw memory -- works even on degraded profiles where pstree/cmdline fail (no kernel symbols needed). Use as a fallback for file metadata when disk MFT is unavailable. If both mftscan and extract_mft_timeline return data, cross-reference timestamps for timestomp detection.
vol_netscan: Returns network connections with PIDs, IPs, and ports. Use to identify C2 channels and lateral movement -- filter by PID to investigate specific processes. If empty, network structures may have been paged out; check disk artifacts (event logs, prefetch) instead.
vol_privileges: Returns process privilege tokens (SeDebugPrivilege, SeImpersonatePrivilege, etc). SeDebugPrivilege on a non-SYSTEM process is highly suspicious -- indicates privilege escalation. Filter by PID; if empty, the process token may have been manipulated.
vol_psscan: Returns ALL processes including terminated and hidden ones (DKOM detection). Use when tool_vol_pstree returns empty or to find processes hidden from the active process tree via EPROCESS unlinking. Compare against pstree output -- any PID here but not there is suspicious.
vol_pstree: Returns process tree with parent-child relationships and PIDs. Call first to identify suspicious processes (wrong parent = evil per Hunt Evil poster). If empty, memory profile may be degraded -- use tool_vol_psscan as fallback.
vol_reg_hivelist: Returns loaded registry hives from memory (SAM, SYSTEM, SOFTWARE, NTUSER.DAT). Use to locate hive offsets needed by other registry plugins or tool_run_regripper. If a hive is missing from results, it may have been unloaded or the memory region was paged out.
vol_sessions: Returns login sessions mapping session IDs to users and processes. Use to identify RDP sessions from unexpected sources (lateral movement indicator). If results show multiple concurrent sessions for one user, investigate for credential compromise.
vol_ssdt: Returns System Service Descriptor Table entries -- hooked entries indicate a rootkit. Run BEFORE trusting any process listing (pstree, psscan). If any entry points outside ntoskrnl/win32k, set a confidence ceiling on all memory-derived findings.
vol_svcscan: Returns Windows services from memory with binary paths and state. Use to find suspicious services or persistence mechanisms -- look for services running from temp directories or with unusual binary paths. If empty, service structures may be paged out; check disk event logs for Event ID 7045 instead.
